title: Summarize SSH Auth Outcomes
hook: SSH logs get easier to read once accepted and failed methods are counted.
problem: You need a quick count of successful and failed SSH authentication methods from an auth log.
command: awk '/sshd/ && /Accepted/ {print "accepted", $7} /sshd/ && /Failed password/ {print "failed", "password"} /sshd/ && /Failed publickey/ {print "failed", "publickey"}' logs/auth.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr
starting_state: A fixture auth log contains accepted public-key logins plus failed password and public-key attempts.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads the auth log and counts matching SSH authentication outcomes.
when_to_use: Use during SSH access triage when you need a fast read on whether noise is password guessing, stale keys, or real accepted access.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat this as a full incident timeline; review the matching source lines before making account or firewall changes.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A count-sorted summary of accepted public-key logins and failed SSH authentication methods.

commands: awk '/sshd/ && /Accepted/ {print "accepted", $7} /sshd/ && /Failed password/ {print "failed", "password"} /sshd/ && /Failed publickey/ {print "failed", "publickey"}' logs/auth.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr

shorts.title: Count SSH auth outcomes.
shorts.voiceover: Before reading every SSH line, count accepted and failed authentication methods so the access pattern is visible.
shorts.caption: Summarize SSH auth before deep triage.

linkedin.question: Do you summarize SSH authentication outcomes before reading every auth log line?
linkedin.cta: Turn noisy SSH logs into a small evidence table first.

youtube.description: Count accepted and failed SSH authentication methods from auth logs.

title: Find SSH Password Auth Exceptions
hook: A global password-auth setting can be changed later by a Match block.
problem: You need to see whether sshd_config has password authentication exceptions under Match rules.
starting_state: An sshd_config fixture disables password authentication globally but enables it for one source range in a Match block.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads sshd_config and prints authentication directives with their current Match context.
when_to_use: Use when a host appears key-only but some users or source ranges can still use password authentication.
when_not_to_use: Do not reload SSH based only on this excerpt; validate the full effective sshd configuration on a real host.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Authentication directives labeled as global or attached to the active Match block.

shorts.title: Find SSH password exceptions.
shorts.voiceover: PasswordAuthentication no is not the whole story if a Match block later changes the rule for a source range.
shorts.caption: Global SSH policy can have exceptions.

linkedin.question: Do you check SSH Match blocks before assuming password auth is disabled everywhere?
linkedin.cta: Read authentication directives with their context, not as isolated lines.

youtube.description: Find password authentication exceptions inside sshd_config Match blocks.

title: List SSH Allow and Deny Rules
hook: SSH access can be shaped by users, groups, and Match blocks.
problem: You need to find SSH allow-list and deny-list directives across the main config and include files.
starting_state: A fixture has AllowUsers in the main sshd_config and DenyUsers plus AllowGroups in an included file.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command searches fixture-local SSH config files and prints matching access directives with file and line numbers.
when_to_use: Use during access reviews, server handoffs, or when a user can authenticate but still cannot open an SSH session.
when_not_to_use: Do not remove allow or deny rules from this output alone; confirm group membership, Match context, and operational ownership first.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Line-numbered SSH access directives from sshd_config and included files.

shorts.title: List SSH access rules.
shorts.voiceover: Search sshd config and includes for allow and deny directives before changing access.
shorts.caption: SSH access rules can hide in includes.

linkedin.question: Do you search sshd_config includes before changing SSH allow-list rules?
linkedin.cta: Review all SSH access directives before editing one file.

youtube.description: Find SSH AllowUsers, AllowGroups, DenyUsers, DenyGroups, and Match directives.

experiments.variant_b: SSH includes can carry access policy.

title: Inventory SSH authorized_keys
hook: authorized_keys files are the practical list of who can use key-based SSH.
problem: You need to list authorized key files and identify the key type plus comment for each entry.
starting_state: Home directories contain authorized_keys files for human and deploy accounts, including one file with loose permissions.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads authorized_keys files and prints the source file, key type, and trailing comment.
when_to_use: Use during SSH access inventory, account handoff, or before removing a user's key-based access.
when_not_to_use: Do not delete keys from this output alone; confirm the comment, owner, fingerprint, and current dependency first.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Each authorized_keys entry with its file path, key type, and comment.

shorts.title: Inventory authorized keys.
shorts.voiceover: List authorized keys by file, key type, and comment so SSH access has an auditable starting point.
shorts.caption: authorized_keys is practical access inventory.

linkedin.question: Do you inventory authorized_keys comments before removing SSH access?
linkedin.cta: Build the key inventory before changing account access.

youtube.description: List authorized_keys entries with file paths, key types, and comments.

title: List Accepted SSH Login Sources
hook: Successful SSH logins are the access events worth anchoring first.
problem: You need to list accepted SSH public-key logins with user and source IP.
starting_state: An auth log contains accepted public-key logins for human and deploy accounts.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads auth.log and prints timestamp, username, and source IP from accepted public-key events.
when_to_use: Use when building an SSH access timeline or checking which accounts had successful key-based logins.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume these are the only access events unless you also search for password, keyboard-interactive, and other Accepted patterns.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Accepted SSH login rows with date, time, user, and source IP.

shorts.title: List accepted SSH sources.
shorts.voiceover: Start SSH triage with accepted logins. Print the timestamp, account, and source IP before chasing failed noise.
shorts.caption: Accepted logins anchor the access timeline.

linkedin.question: Do you anchor SSH triage on successful logins before reviewing failed attempts?
linkedin.cta: List accepted SSH sources before changing access.

youtube.description: List successful SSH public-key logins with users and source IPs.

title: Show Failed SSH Public-Key Users
hook: A failed public-key attempt often points to stale keys or the wrong account.
problem: You need to extract users and source IPs from failed SSH public-key attempts.
command: awk '/Failed publickey/ {print $9, $11}' logs/auth.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr
starting_state: An auth log contains a failed public-key attempt for a deploy account.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads auth.log and counts failed public-key attempts by user and source IP.
when_to_use: Use when a key-based SSH login fails and you need to separate stale-key failures from password guessing.
when_not_to_use: Do not rotate or remove keys based on this count alone; inspect key fingerprints and account ownership first.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Counted failed public-key attempts grouped by username and source IP.

commands: awk '/Failed publickey/ {print $9, $11}' logs/auth.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr

shorts.title: Find failed SSH keys.
shorts.voiceover: Filter failed public-key events to see which account and source are trying a key that does not work.
shorts.caption: Failed public-key is different from password noise.

linkedin.question: Do you separate failed public-key SSH attempts from password guessing?
linkedin.cta: Treat stale-key evidence differently from brute-force noise.

youtube.description: Extract users and sources from failed SSH public-key attempts.

title: Summarize SSH Authorized Key Types
hook: Key inventory gets more useful when old key types stand out.
problem: You need to count SSH key algorithms used in authorized_keys files.
command: find home -path '*/.ssh/authorized_keys' -exec awk '{print $1}' {} + | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr
starting_state: Authorized_keys files contain a mix of ed25519 and rsa entries.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads authorized_keys files and counts the first field, which is the key type.
when_to_use: Use during SSH access reviews when you need to spot legacy key types before planning cleanup.
when_not_to_use: Do not delete RSA keys solely because they appear here; confirm policy, fingerprint, owner, and compatibility first.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A count-sorted list of authorized SSH key types.

commands: find home -path '*/.ssh/authorized_keys' -exec awk '{print $1}' {} + | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr

shorts.title: Count SSH key types.
shorts.voiceover: Count authorized key algorithms so old or unexpected key types stand out during access review.
shorts.caption: Key type summary before key cleanup.

linkedin.question: Do you summarize authorized SSH key types before planning key cleanup?
linkedin.cta: Use key type counts as inventory, not as an automatic delete list.

youtube.description: Count key algorithms across authorized_keys files.

title: Find Loose authorized_keys Modes
hook: SSH key access files should not be looser than intended.
problem: You need to find authorized_keys files with permissions broader than 600.
starting_state: One authorized_keys file is mode 644 while the others are mode 600.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command lists authorized_keys modes and filters files whose numeric mode is greater than 600.
when_to_use: Use during SSH access audits or after provisioning user keys to spot loose access-file permissions.
when_not_to_use: Do not chmod files blindly from this list; confirm ownership, platform expectations, and deployment tooling first.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Mode and path for authorized_keys files with permissions broader than 600.

shorts.title: Find loose authorized_keys.
shorts.voiceover: List authorized_keys modes and flag files broader than 600 before changing SSH access.
shorts.caption: Review loose SSH access-file modes.

linkedin.question: Do you check authorized_keys permissions during SSH access reviews?
linkedin.cta: Find loose modes before editing keys or accounts.

youtube.description: Find authorized_keys files with permissions broader than 600.

title: Show SSH Auth Policy Order
hook: The order of Include, Match, and authentication directives changes how SSH policy reads.
problem: You need to see SSH authentication directives in file order, including Include and Match lines.
starting_state: An sshd_config fixture has global authentication settings, an Include directive, and a Match block with overrides.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints line-numbered policy directives in the order sshd_config presents them.
when_to_use: Use when SSH policy looks contradictory and you need to see whether later Match rules override global assumptions.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat grep output as a full effective config on production systems; use sshd's config test tooling where available.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Line-numbered Include, Match, and authentication directives from sshd_config.

shorts.title: Read SSH policy in order.
shorts.voiceover: Print Include, Match, and authentication directives in file order so later exceptions are visible.
shorts.caption: SSH policy order matters.

linkedin.question: Do you read SSH authentication policy in file order when Match blocks are present?
linkedin.cta: Check policy order before editing sshd_config.

youtube.description: Show SSH authentication policy directives in file order.

title: Extract SSH AllowUsers Accounts
hook: AllowUsers turns SSH access into an explicit account list.
problem: You need to extract each account named in an AllowUsers directive.
command: awk '/^AllowUsers/ {for (i = 2; i <= NF; i++) print $i}' etc/ssh/sshd_config
starting_state: The SSH daemon config contains an AllowUsers directive for alice and deploy.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads sshd_config and prints each allowed account on its own line.
when_to_use: Use during account reviews, server handoffs, or when checking whether a user is excluded by SSH allow-list policy.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume this is the complete access list if AllowGroups, Match blocks, PAM, or cloud-side controls also apply.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: One SSH AllowUsers account per line.

commands: awk '/^AllowUsers/ {for (i = 2; i <= NF; i++) print $i}' etc/ssh/sshd_config

shorts.title: Extract SSH AllowUsers.
shorts.voiceover: Split AllowUsers into one account per line so the SSH allow-list is easy to review.
shorts.caption: Make the SSH allow-list readable.

linkedin.question: Do you extract SSH AllowUsers into a reviewable account list?
linkedin.cta: Turn access directives into a simple checklist before editing.

youtube.description: Extract accounts from an SSH AllowUsers directive.
